title: Hashes in the Breach File
description:
Amaka drops a copy of the staging server's password file on your desk. 'A junior engineer asked me which of these accounts we need to rotate first. Good question. The hashes tell you what you need to know. Which of the following is the most urgent to rotate, and why?'

Account A: $2b$12$eImiTXuWVxfM37uY4JANjQ==.dF9A77h0uJjHBA9I.N5SZbHfxbsOG
Account B: 5f4dcc3b5aa765d61d8327deb882cf99
Account C: 5e884898da28047151d0e56f8dc6292773603d0d6aabbdd62a11ef721d1542d8
Account D: $argon2id$v=19$m=65536,t=3,p=4$c29tZXNhbHQ$RdescudvJCsgt3ub+b+dWRWJTmaaJObG

choices: Account B — an unsalted MD5 of a common password; a modern GPU can crack this against a public rainbow table in seconds.
Account A — bcrypt is obsolete; modern attackers bypass it routinely.
Account D — argon2id produces long hashes which suggests it is slow, and slow means cheap to brute force.
Account C — SHA-256 is a cryptographic hash and any cryptographic hash that long can be reversed by GPU farms within hours.
Account B — an unsalted MD5 of a common password; a modern GPU can crack this against a public rainbow table in seconds.
Account A — bcrypt is obsolete; modern attackers bypass it routinely.
Account D — argon2id produces long hashes which suggests it is slow, and slow means cheap to brute force.
Account C — SHA-256 is a cryptographic hash and any cryptographic hash that long can be reversed by GPU farms within hours.
